step.title: Step 22
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT should move charcoal F left')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 12 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 94, level 2.

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 12 of about 50 target actions so far.
STALL: the board has not changed for your last 8 actions on this level — those actions had no visible effect. Stop repeating the same move; try a different action or target.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 8 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see what happened. The charcoal F-shape is now at rows 21-29, cols 39-47, with only 45 pixels (was 72 before). It's partially overlapping with the black F-shape and the divider strip. The hash changed from da9403ac2e41f12e to 3f302d594a8175e7, which means the shape changed (it got clipped or merged).
Also, I notice the divider strip (S color) is now at cols 48-50, split into two parts: rows 0-17 and rows 21-62. The charcoal F-shape is blocking part of it.
Key observations:
1. The charcoal F-shape moved from cols 15-29 to cols 39-47 (moved RIGHT by 24 cols, not left!)
2. The black F-shape stayed at cols 45-59 (didn't move)
3. DOWN didn't work (8 stalling actions)
4. The charcoal F-shape's hash changed, meaning its shape was modified
Wait, this is very different from what I expected. RIGHT moved the charcoal F to the RIGHT, not left. And the black F didn't move at all.
Let me reconsider the action model for Level 2:
- In Level 1, LEFT/RIGHT moved both F-shapes in opposite directions
- In Level 2, RIGHT seems to only move the charcoal F to the right (and it got clipped when overlapping with the black F/divider)
- DOWN doesn't seem to do anything
Actually, looking more carefully, the charcoal F moved from cols 15-29 to cols 39-47. That's a shift of +24 cols (right by 24). With 4 RIGHT actions, that's 6 cols per RIGHT action.
But wait, the charcoal F's pixels went from 72 to 45, and its hash changed. This means part of the F-shape was clipped or merged with something. The boundary shows it's now at rows 21-29 (was 18-29), so the top part was clipped.
I think what happened is:
1. RIGHT moves the charcoal F to the right (not left as I thought)
2. The charcoal F collided with the divider strip and got partially clipped
3. DOWN doesn't move anything
This is a completely different action model than Level 1. Let me reconsider.
Actually, wait. In Level 1, I pressed LEFT to move the charcoal F to the right (toward the yellow F). So LEFT moved the charcoal F right. In Level 2, I pressed RIGHT, and the charcoal F moved right. So the action model might be:
- LEFT: moves charcoal F left, black F left (both left)
- RIGHT: moves charcoal F right, black F right (both right)
No, that doesn't match Level 1 either. Let me re-examine Level 1.
In Level 1:
- Initial: black F at cols 15-23, charcoal F at cols 36-44
- After LEFT x14: black F at cols 3-11 (moved left by 12), charcoal F at cols 51-59 (moved right by 15)
Wait, that's 14 LEFT actions. Black F moved from 15 to 3 (left by 12 cols in 14 actions = ~0.86 cols per action). Charcoal F moved from 36 to 51 (right by 15 cols in 14 actions = ~1.07 cols per action).
Hmm, the movement isn't uniform. Let me reconsider.
Actually, in Level 1, the F-shapes moved 3 cols per action. Black F: 15 → 3 is 12 cols left in 4 actions (if only 4 LEFT actions were needed). But I pressed 14 LEFT actions. So maybe the movement is 3 cols per action, but the black F hit a boundary and stopped moving after 4 actions (15 - 4*3 = 3).
Wait, let me reconsider. In Level 1:
- Black F initial: cols 15-23
- Charcoal F initial: cols 36-44
- After LEFT x14: charcoal F at cols 51-59 (moved right by 15 cols), black F at cols 3-11 (moved left by 12 cols)
If LEFT moves charcoal F right by 3 cols per action and black F left by 3 cols per action:
- Charcoal F: 36 + 14*3 = 78 (but it's at 51, so it stopped at 51)
- Black F: 15 - 14*3 = -27 (but it's at 3, so it stopped at 3)
Hmm, that doesn't work either. Maybe the movement is 1 col per action:
- Charcoal F: 36 + 14 = 50 (close to 51)
- Black F: 15 - 14 = 1 (close to 3)
Still not exact. Let me think differently.
Actually, I think the key insight is that in Level 1, the F-shapes move toward/away from each other. LEFT moves them apart, RIGHT moves them together. The charcoal F is the "active" one that gets positioned to overlap the target.
In Level 2, the situation is different. The charcoal F and black F are on opposite sides of the divider. The yellow F is at the bottom left.
Looking at the current state:
- Charcoal F: rows 21-29, cols 39-47 (partially clipped, 45 pixels)
- Black F: rows 18-29, cols 45-59 (unchanged)
- Yellow F: rows 42-53, cols 3-17 (unchanged)
The charcoal F is now overlapping with the black F and the divider. It seems like the charcoal F got stuck.
I need to figure out:
1. How to move the charcoal F down to the yellow F's position (rows 42-53)
2. How to move the charcoal F left to the yellow F's position (cols 3-17)
Since DOWN didn't work, maybe I need to use a different approach. Or maybe the charcoal F needs to be in a certain position before it can move vertically.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics. In Level 1:
- The charcoal F was moved horizontally to overlap the yellow F
- Both were at the same vertical position (rows 45-53)
- No vertical movement was needed
In Level 2:
- The charcoal F is at rows 18-29, yellow F is at rows 42-53
- Vertical movement IS needed
- But DOWN didn't work
Maybe the game requires the charcoal F to first be positioned horizontally, and then it can be moved vertically. Or maybe the charcoal F needs to be in a specific column range before it can move vertically.
Or maybe the action model is different:
- LEFT/RIGHT move the charcoal F horizontally
- UP/DOWN move the black F vertically (or some other element)
- SPACE or MOUSE triggers the actual placement
Let me try a different approach. Let me first undo the damage by moving the charcoal F back to its original position, then try a different strategy.
Actually, the charcoal F is now clipped (45 pixels instead of 72). It might not be possible to restore it. Let me try pressing LEFT to move it back left.
Wait, but the hash changed, which means the shape is different. It might not be possible to restore the original shape.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal in Level 2 is not to move the charcoal F to the yellow F, but to do something else entirely.
Looking at the current state:
- The charcoal F is partially overlapping with the black F and the divider
- The black F has blue dots
- The yellow F is at the bottom left
Maybe the goal is to:
1. Move the black F (with blue dots) to overlap the yellow F
2. The blue dots turn yellow when they overlap
3. All dots need to be yellow to complete the level
But the black F has stem on RIGHT, and the yellow F has stem on LEFT. They're mirror images, so they won't fully overlap.
Or maybe the goal is to:
1. Position the charcoal F at the divider
2. Press SPACE to "transfer" the charcoal F to the yellow F's position
3. The charcoal F replaces the yellow F
Let me try pressing SPACE to see what happens.
Actually, let me first try to understand what happened to the charcoal F. It moved from cols 15-29 to cols 39-47 after RIGHT x4. That's 24 cols right in 4 actions, or 6 cols per action. But the F-shape width is 15 cols (29-15+1=15), and it's now at cols 39-47, which is 9 cols wide. So it got clipped.
I think the charcoal F hit the divider strip and got partially absorbed. The divider is at cols 48-50 (originally cols 36-38 in Level 1, but it seems to have moved).
Let me try pressing LEFT to move the charcoal F back left, away from the divider.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The charcoal F moved RIGHT (not left!) and got clipped by the divider. DOWN didn't work. Need to rethink the action model for Level 2. Let me try LEFT to move the charcoal F back.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to move charcoal F back left
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT should move charcoal F left')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  Charcoal F: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")

print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
